coredata.dc:title: N,S-codoped carbon dots as deposition regulating electrolyte additive for stable lithium metal anode
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Shuo
Luo, Zheng
Tu, Hanyu
Zhang, Hao
Deng, Weina
Zou, Guoqiang
Hou, Hongshuai
Ji, Xiaobo
coredata.dc:description: Lithium metal anode is widely studied owing to its feature of ultrahigh theoretical specific capacity with lowest electrochemical potential. Nevertheless, the inhomogeneous deposition of Li+ ions is apt to accelerate the emergence of dendritic Li, which is a brake on the practical application of lithium metal batteries. To solve this issue, herein, we propose nitrogen, sulfur-codoped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) as the deposition regulating additives of electrolyte to guide lithium ion homogeneous deposition. Within the electrolyte, N,S-CDs additives can absorb the surrounding Li+ ions and deposit homogeneously on the current collector, resulting in a uniform lithium deposition layer. As expected, at the presence of 0.3 mg/mL N,S-CDs, the long-term cycling stability over 1200 h and 300 h with low polarization are achieved both in the Li|Li symmetric cell at current density of 1.0 and 3.0 mA cm−2, respectively. Meanwhile, there is an obvious contrast between the capacity retention in the Li-S full cell between the cell with and without N,S-CDs. In addition, it is worth noting that the beneficial effect of N,S-CDs for stable lithium metal anode is firstly demonstrated by means of confocal laser scanning microscope as well.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium metal anode
Electrolyte additives
Lithium dendrite
Lithium-sulfur battery
Carbon dots

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 52074359 , 51904342 ), National Key Research and Development Program of China ( 2018YFB0104200 ), Hunan Provincial Science and Technology Plan ( 2020JJ3048 ), the Science and Technology Innovation Program of Hunan Province ( 2020RC4005 , 2019RS1004 ), Innovation Mover Program of Central South University ( 2020CX007 ), and Fundamental Research Funds for Central Universities of the Central South University ( 2020zzts393 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic depicting the effect of CDs for stable lithium metal anode. (b) The model structures of binding energy between Li ions and distinct lithiophilic site by means of DFT calculation.

(a) Schematic depicting the mechanism for the formation of N,S-CDs. (b) The TEM images of the N,S-CDs. (c) The FTIR pattern of the N,S-CDs. (d-g) The XPS full survey of the N,S-CDs and high-resolution XPS of C 1s, N 1s, and S 2p spectra of the N,S-CDs, respectively. (h) Digital photos of conventional ether electrolyte with different concentration (0.1-0.8 mg/mL) of the N,S-CDs.

(a) Initial galvanostatic voltage curve of Li|Cu half-cell with and without N,S-CDs additives. (b-c) Voltage-time (b) and voltage-current curve (c) at different current from 20 µA to 120 µA of the Li|Li symmetric cell with and without N,S-CDs additives. (d) SEM images of Li+ ion deposition on the copper foil without (d1, d2) and with (d3, d4) N,S-CDs additives added at 0.5 mA cm−2 with an areal capacity of 5 mA h cm−2, top view (d1, d3) and cross-sectional view (d2, d4). (e) Confocal luminescence images of Li deposition layer without (e1, e5) and with N,S-CDs at 0.5 mA cm−2 with an areal capacity of 1.0 (e2, e6), 3.0 (e3, e7), and 5.0 mAh cm−2 (e4, e8), respectively, in dark field (e1-e4) and compound field (e5-e8).

Long-term cycling performance of N,S-CDs additives. (a-b) Coulombic efficiency of Li|Cu half-cell with and without N,S-CDs additives at 0.5 mA cm−2 (a) and 1.0 mA cm−2 (b) with a capacity of 1.0 mAh cm−2. (c-e) Voltage-time profile of in the Li|Li symmetric cell with and without N,S-CDs additives at 1.0 mA cm−2 (c), 3.0 mA cm−2 (d) with a capacity of 1.0 mAh cm−2 and rate performance (e).

(a) The SEM image of Li+ ion deposition in the Li|Li symmetric cell at 1.0 mA cm−2 with an areal capacity of 1.0 mA h cm−2. The top view (a1-a3) and cross-sectional view (a4-a6) of the cell without N,S-CDs additives. The top view (a7-a9) and cross-sectional view (a10-a12) of the cell with N,S-CDs additives. (b-d) Nyquist plot (b-c) and their results (d) of the impedance spectra behavior depending on the cycle progress at current density of 3.0 mA cm−2.

Cycle performance of Li-S batteries with and without at various rates (1 C = 1675 mAh g−1). (a-b) Cycle performance (a) and charge-discharge curves (b) at 0.5 C. (c) Cycle performance at 1.0 C. (d-f) Cycle performance (d) and charge-discharge curves (e-f) at various rates (0.2 C, 0.5 C, 1.0 C, and 2.0 C). (g) SEM images of Li metal deposition in Li-S full cell with (e) and without (f) N,S-CDs after rate testing, the inset: high magnification image, correspondingly.

head.ce:title: N,S-codoped carbon dots as deposition regulating electrolyte additive for stable lithium metal anode

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Powder Metallurgy, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University Changsha, 410083 China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Powder Metallurgy
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aState Key Laboratory of Powder Metallurgy, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University Changsha, 410083 China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hunan Key Laboratory of Applied Environmental Photocatalysis, Changsha University, Changsha 410022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hunan Key Laboratory of Applied Environmental Photocatalysis

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bHunan Key Laboratory of Applied Environmental Photocatalysis, Changsha University, Changsha 410022, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lithium metal anode is widely studied owing to its feature of ultrahigh theoretical specific capacity with lowest electrochemical potential. Nevertheless, the inhomogeneous deposition of Li+ ions is apt to accelerate the emergence of dendritic Li, which is a brake on the practical application of lithium metal batteries. To solve this issue, herein, we propose nitrogen, sulfur-codoped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) as the deposition regulating additives of electrolyte to guide lithium ion homogeneous deposition. Within the electrolyte, N,S-CDs additives can absorb the surrounding Li+ ions and deposit homogeneously on the current collector, resulting in a uniform lithium deposition layer. As expected, at the presence of 0.3 mg/mL N,S-CDs, the long-term cycling stability over 1200 h and 300 h with low polarization are achieved both in the Li|Li symmetric cell at current density of 1.0 and 3.0 mA cm−2, respectively. Meanwhile, there is an obvious contrast between the capacity retention in the Li-S full cell between the cell with and without N,S-CDs. In addition, it is worth noting that the beneficial effect of N,S-CDs for stable lithium metal anode is firstly demonstrated by means of confocal laser scanning microscope as well.

ce:section.ce:para: Compared to the graphite anode in lithium ion batteries (LIBs), lithium metal anode (LMA) exhibits numerous outstanding merits (e.g. high theoretical specific capacity of 3860 mAh g−1, the lowest negative redox potential of −3.040 V versus a standard hydrogen electrode, as well as low gravimetric density of 0.534 g cm−3), and thus it is deemed as the “Holy Grail” of lithium ion batteries [1]. Nevertheless, the notorious dendritic Li growth induced by thermodynamics instability and ultrahigh activity of lithium metal anode, which results in short circuit and even severe explosive accidents, stunts critically the development and practical applications of lithium metal batteries (LMBs) [2-4]. Consequently, the question “how to effectively suppress the continuous growth of Li dendrites” is critical for optimizing the performance of LMBs [5].

Based on the Sand's time model, the growth rate of dendritic Li is inversely related to electric field strength [6]. Gradually speaking, Li+ ions will not deposit homogeneously on the traditional current collector (e.g., copper foil [7]) due to their nature in lithiophobic. Instead, Li+ ions are apt to nucleate randomly on the current collector and further growth as dendritic Li driven by thermodynamic instability and uneven electric field [8]. Therefore, numerous prominent strategies were proposed to regulate electric field distribution on LMB during cycling, including engineering current collectors [9-12], using solid-state electrolyte [13-16] as well as incorporating electrolyte additives [17-19]. Compared to the former, incorporating electrolyte additives, which are acclaimed as “vitamin” of batteries, is widely considered as a facile but cost-effective strategy to suppress dendric Li growth, and thus the research interests of electrolyte additives have surged recently [20, 21]. In terms of electrolyte additives, it can be divided mainly into two categories: SEI forming additives and deposition regulating additives. The former mainly aims at building a robust, stable SEI layers [22, 23], whereas the latter is beneficial for regulating the deposition process of Li+ ions [8, 24]. The SEI forming additives are gradually limited by drawbacks of irreversible interaction with LMA during long-term cycling, resulting in the capacity decrease of batteries [20, 25]. Therefore, many deposition regulating additives, especially carbon-based nanomaterials with numerous ideal merits (e.g., high lithiophility and large specific surface area) [26], have been proposed in succession and some satisfactory results were obtained. For instance, Zhang et al.[27] proposed that nanodiamond (ND) severed as deposition regulating additives to achieve a homogeneous deposition layer owing to the fact that ND could absorb Li+ ions and further co-deposit on the Cu foil. Likewise, 2D vermiculite sheets [28] and nitrofullerene [29] were proposed as deposition regulating additives to guide Li+ ion homogeneous deposition as well. Carbon dots (CDs) with ultrasmall size of < 10 nm and rich functional groups have been applied widely in various fields [30-34]. Very recently, CDs have been introduced as alternatives of the above additives [35, 36]. For instance, Hu et al.[35] presented that graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can server as the nucleation seeds of Li+ ion on the electrode surface, and then the homogeneous electrical field as well as ion flux was obtained near the electrode-electrolyte interface. Unfortunately, most of the above additives exist nonnegligible drawbacks of high-cost and poor dispersity in the nonaqueous electrolyte system, largely limiting their practical applications. Therefore, exploring an ideal electrolyte additive to avoid these drawbacks is in urgent demand.

Herein, we proposed carbon dots with excellent dispersity in electrolyte solution and high yield as electrolyte additives to suppress dendritic Li growth. Additionally, enormous studies suggested that heteroatoms doping enabled to modify the performance of carbon materials (e.g., the Li+ adsorption ability) [27, 37]. For instance, recently, Wang et al.[38] reported that nitrogen, sulfur-codoped double-vacancy defects rendered ultrahigh Li adsorption energy of 6.56 eV. Based on this, we discussed the feasibility of nitrogen, sulfur-codoped CDs (N,S-CDs) as deposition regulating additive by means of Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations. Furthermore, the N,S-CDs with excellent dispersity in electrolyte solution was prepared. Within untreated electrolyte, Li+ ions are apt to deposit randomly during cycle process, resulting in the emergence of Li dendrite formation. On the contrary, N,S-CDs with numerous lithiophilic sites can adsorb Li+ ions on their surface, and further co-deposit homogeneously on the matrix during plating process ( Fig. 1
a). Therefore, a smooth and dense Li deposition layer was achieved at the present of N,S-CDs, resulting in a stable long-term cycling performance in the Li|Li symmetric cell and a high capacity retention in the Li-S full-cell.

The interaction between Li ions and N,S-containing lithiophilic site was analyzed by means of the DFT calculation for verifying our guess. To be convenient for calculation, the four types of N,S-containing functional groups (pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N, graphitic-N and sulfur atoms) were chosen, and the corresponding model structures for the DFT calculation of binding energy are shown in Fig. 1
b. Analysis suggested that graphitic-N exhibited highest binding energy with Li+ ion of -1.92 eV, which was slightly better than that of pyrrolic-N (-1.81 eV) and pyridinic (-1.90 eV). Although the sulfur atoms sites exhibited a relatively weaker interaction with Li+ ion than N-containing functional groups, it remained adsorption ability of Li+ ion due to the negative value of binding energy (-0.71 eV). Based on this, N,S-CDs could be competent to act as deposition regulating additives, and achieve an uniform deposition of Li+ ions.

According to the design above, the N,S-CDs was fabricated by typical aldol condensation as shown in Fig. 2
a, in which acetaldehyde and thiourea was chosen as carbon source owing to its good reactivity. It was worth noting that the N,S-CDs powders could be well dispersed in common organic solvent (e.g. ethanol, methanol, N-Methyl pyrrolidone, N,N-dimethylformamide and Dichloromethane) (Fig. S1, Supporting Information), which was significantly different from most of previous reported carbon dots [35]. There are three absorption bands in the UV-vis absorption spectra of the N,S-CDs solution (Fig. S2, Supporting Information), which are corresponding π−π* transition of conjugated C=C, n-π* transition of carbon-heteroatom double bond and the surface state of N,S-CDs, respectively [35, 39]. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images rendered that the N,S-CDs with the size of 1.5-3.0 nm were well dispersed ( Fig. 2
b). The results of X-ray diffraction (XRD) were also utilized to characterize the crystal structure of CDs with a broad peak at around 20°, indicating a highly amorphous carbon phase (Fig. S3, Supporting Information). The results of Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FITR) suggest that there are various functional groups on the surface of N,S-CDs ( Fig. 2
c). Thereinto, the broad absorption band around 3400 and 3200 cm−1 are attributed to stretching vibrations of −O−H and −N−H, respectively [40]. The strong peaks around 2956∼2918 and 1450∼1372 cm−1 are related to the stretching and bending vibration of carbon-hydrogen single bond (−C−H) of methyl groups, respectively, resulting in good lipophilicity of N,S-CDs [41]. The strong peak around 2044 cm−1 is attributed to −SCN group [40], whereas the peaks at 1652, 1522, 1192 cm−1 are ascribed to stretching vibration of −C=O, −C=C and −C−N, respectively [42]. The chemical compositions and structures of N,S-CDs were further explored by XPS. The full spectra presented four typical peaks at S 2p (163 eV), C 1s (285 eV), N 1s (399 eV), and O 1s (532 eV), of which content is 5.52%, 77.98%, 11.93% and 4.56%, respectively ( Fig. 2
d). The high-resolution C 1s spectra revealed four peaks at 284.80, 285.78, 286.43, and 288.20 eV, corresponding to sp2 C in graphene (C−C), sp3 C in C−N/C−S/C−O, CN and C=O, respectively ( Fig. 2
e) [39, 43]. The high-resolution N 1s spectra revealed four peaks at 397.3, 399.4, and 401.5 eV, which are assigned to pyridinic-N, and pyrrolic-N, graphitic-N respectively ( Fig. 2
f) [44]. In the high-resolution S 2p spectra ( Fig. 2
g), the bond can be deconvoluted into three peaks, and the peaks at 161.63 and 162.93 eV are attributed to C-S covalent bond of S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2, whereas the peak at 167.93 eV corresponds to sulfone bridges (−C−SOx−C−) [45, 46]. The above XPS and FITR results agree well with our design principle, and further indicated that the as-prepared materials are N,S-CDs as well. The N,S-CDs can be well dispersed in ether electrolyte ( Fig. 2
h), which is a fundamental precondition for their application in electrolyte.

A series of kinetics results confirmed the beneficial role of N,S-CDs for Li+ ion homogeneous deposition. As shown in Fig. 3
a, the Li|Cu half cell without N,S-CDs show the a large initial nucleation overpotential of 78 mV, whereas it was downgraded to 63 mV at the presence of N,S-CDs. It can be attributed to the fact that N,S-CDs can serve as nucleation site of Li+ ions so that the energy barriers for nucleation process can be decreased significantly. In addition, the exchange current density (i0 ) was obtained in the Li|Li symmetric cells according to Equation (1) [47]:
i =
i 0
F
R T
η
t o t
2
where F and R refer to Faraday's constant (96485.33 C/mol) and molar gas constant (8.314 J/(mol•K)), respectively. ηtot and i refer to the total overpotential and current density, respectively, whereas T refers to the testing temperature (298.15 K). As shown in Fig. 3
b and c, Li|Li symmetric cell without the N,S-CDs additives exhibited unsatisfactory interfacial kinetics and then a low exchange current density of 0.136 mA was obtained. On the contrary, at the presence of N,S-CDs, a high exchange current density of 0.423 mA was achieved, which suggested the presence of N,S-CDs was beneficial for charge transfer of Li|Li symmetric cell, and agreed well with previous reports [24, 48].

To further prove aforementioned mechanism, the SEM image of Li deposition morphology on the copper current collector with and without N,S-CDs added after initial cycle at 0.5 mA cm−2 with an areal capacity of 1.0, 3.0 and 5.0 mAh cm−2 were illustrated in Fig. 3
d and Fig. S4 (Supporting Information), respectively. As for Li deposition in the conventional ether electrolyte, numerous pine needle-like lithium grew gradually with the increase of areal capacity due to the lithiophobic nature of Cu foil. Meanwhile, lithium deposition layer was very porous and its thickness reached ∼60 μm from the cross-sectional view ( Fig. 3
d2), which suggested excessive consumption of electrolyte and low utilization of active lithium. In strong contrast, a more uniform and denser deposition layer was achieved at the presence of N,S-CDs additives, and then the thickness of lithium deposition layer reduced to ∼35 μm as well. Owing to common fluorescent character of carbon dots, the existence of N,S-CDs additives was testified vividly by means of confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM). As shown in Fig. 3
e, little fluorescent signal was observed in the CLSM images of lithium deposition layer within the conventional ether electrolyte. However, there are numerous yellowish-green fluorescent signal on the lithium deposition layer with N,S-CDs at 0.5 mA cm−2 with an areal capacity of 1.0, 3.0, 5.0 mAh cm−2, which were rinsed to remove the residual electrolyte by DME before observation, respectively. In term of stripping process, there were obvious fluorescent signal on the deposition layer (initial lithium loading capability of 3 mAh cm−2) when stripping capacity was 1 mAh cm−2, whereas little fluorescent signal was found when stripping capacity increased to 3 mAh cm−2. The above result suggests that N,S-CDs could be reused in electrolyte during the Li platting/stripping processes, and thus achieved a long-term stability of cell (Fig. S5, Supporting Information). Additionally, the lithium deposition layer after soaking with N,S-CDs containing electrolyte for ten minutes was chosen as control sample to further prove that fluorescent signal originated from N,S-CDs co-deposition on current collector rather than residual N,S-CDs containing electrolyte. After rinsing by DME to remove the residual electrolyte, there is also little fluorescent signal on this control sample as expected (Fig. S6, Supporting Information). Meanwhile, the aforementioned results proved convincingly that N,S-CDs was beneficial for suppressing dendritic lithium growth via governing lithium deposition behavior rather than self-decomposition for SEI layer formation. However, it should be also noted that the images of CLSM cannot exhibit quantitatively the number of N,S-CDs because the thickness of lithium deposition layer influenced significantly fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs.

To explore the effect of N,S-CDs on coulombic efficiency (CE) of lithium plating and stripping, Li|Cu half cells with different concentration of 0-0.8 mg/mL of N,S-CDs in electrolyte solution were assembled. The CE of lithium plating and stripping with different concentration of N,S-CDs at different current densities are presented in Fig. 4
a, b and Fig. S7 (Supporting Information). Clearly, the CE performance of Li|Cu half cells increased at first and then decreased when the concentration over 0.3 mg/mL, which may be ascribed to the fact that the presence of excessive N,S-CDs increases the viscosity of electrolyte system so that blocks the Li+ ions flux. Li|Cu half cells with an optimum condition (0.3 mg/mL) exhibited high average CE over 200 cycles (0.5 mA cm−2) and 120 cycles (1.0 mA cm−2), respectively. In contrast, the CE of Li|Cu half cells in the conventional ether electrolyte attenuated rapidly after 120 and 60 cycles, respectively. The results above suggested that the presence of N,S-CDs is beneficial for governing Li deposition process.

Li|Li symmetric cell test was used to investigate the role of N,S-CDs in enhancing long-term cycle stability. As shown in Fig. 4
c-d, Li|Li symmetric cell with N,S-CDs added exhibited very stable performance over 1200 h (600 cycles) and 300 h (∼450 cycles) at 1.0 and 3.0 mA cm−2 with an areal capacity of 1.0 mAh cm−2, respectively. However, the cell within the conventional ether electrolyte exhibited large voltage hysteresis after 600 h and 70 h at 1.0 mA cm−2 and 3.0 mA cm−2, respectively, which suggested the exhaustion of electrolyte as well as the emergence of dead Li. In comparison with Li|Li symmetric cell in the conventional ether electrolyte, a lower and more stable voltage hysteresis were achieved due to the effect of N,S-CDs for regulating Li+ ions flux. When further evaluated the rate performance of Li|Li symmetric cell with and without N,S-CDs ( Fig. 4
e), the presence of N,S-CDs always induced the lowest voltage hysteresis even at high current density of 5 mA cm−2, which suggested the superiority of N,S-CDs on enhancing the long-term cycle stability of lithium metal anode. Besides, the SEM images of Li morphology after various cycle times was another proof for the benefit of N,S-CDs for Li+ ions deposition ( Fig. 5
a). Within the conventional ether electrolyte, numerous cracks and holes were formed on the Li deposition layer solely after 10th cycle ( Fig. 5
a1 and a4), which was attributed to the emergence of homogeneous lithium nuclei on lithium anode [49]. Moreover, the Li deposition layer was further disordered and then dead Li emerged clearly in the cross-section during 20th cycle, resulting in the poor reversibility of metallic Li. ( Fig. 5
a2 and a5). After 30th cycle, the lithium dendrite and dead Li can be clearly observed on the lithium metal anode, resulting in a porous and irregular Li deposition layer ( Fig. 5
a3 and a6). Within the N,S-CDs containing electrolyte, on the contrary, there was little dendritic Li and dead Li on the Li deposition layer during cycling, and the thickness of the Li deposition layer always keep about 10 μm, which suggested that N,S-CDs can guide Li+ ions to homogeneously deposit and thus suppress the growth of dendritic Li ( Fig. 5
a7-a12). The similar results were observed on the surface of lithium metal anode after rate cycling as well (Fig. S8, Supporting Information). Moreover, impedances of the Li|Li symmetric cells with and without N,S-CDs depending on the cycle progress were tested to further verify the results above. As shown in Fig. 5
b-d, both sets of curves included two semicircles in high frequencies and medium frequencies, which referred to the resistance of surface film (Rsf) as well as the charge transfer resistance (Rct), respectively [9, 50]. During cycle process, the resistance of the cell both with and without N,S-CDs decreased at first because the oxide layer on the bulk lithium was gradually replaced by Li+ conductive reduction products. However, without the presence of N,S-CDs in the electrolyte, the Rsf of Li||Li symmetric cells increased suddenly after 150th cycle, which results form the unstable SEI layer and emergency of dead Li. In contrast, the cell with N,S-CDs adding maintains a more lower and stable impedance after 10th cycle, indicating the beneficial effect of N,S-CDs for stable lithium metal anode. Furthermore, we noticed the resistance of bulk electrolyte of cell increased gradually, because the liquid electrolyte is continuously consumed [51].

The potential viability in practical batteries of N,S-CDs as an electrolyte additives for stable lithium metal anode was tested in the lithium metal batteries, in which a sulfur composite cathode was chosen to match with lithium metal anode. Fig. 6
a rendered that the presence of N,S-CDs additives can enhance significantly the cycle stability of Li-S cell at 0.5 C (1 C=1675 mAh g−1) after 0.05 C activation for one cycle. In the initial cycles, Li-S cells with and without N,S-CDs showed nearly the same reversible capacity (∼915 mAh g−1) and Coulombic efficiency of ∼97 %. However, the capacity of the Li-S cell in the conventional ether electrolyte dropped very fast and the capacity decreased below 500 mA h g−1 after 300 cycle, whereas the Li-S cell with N,S-CDs added exhibited more stable performance and maintained about 600 mAh g−1 after 300 cycles. The charge–discharge voltage plateaus at different cycles also rendered intuitively the beneficial effect of N,S-CDs for capacity retention of Li-S cell ( Fig. 6
b). The difference of cycling performance was more significant between the Li-S cell with and without N,S-CDs additives at 1.0 C. As shown in Fig. 6
c, the capability of Li-S cell can be upgraded from 491 to ∼600 mAh/g after 200 cycles at the presence of N,S-CDs. Meanwhile, it should be noted that there were momentary process of capacity increasing both of the Li-S cell with and without N,S-CDs in the initial several cycles, which might be caused by insufficient activation process. The performance of rate capability was also investigated as shown in Fig. 6
d-f, the Li-S cell with and without N,S-CDs exhibited similar performance initially, which might result from the slow growth of Li dendrite at low discharge current density. However, the discharge capacity of Li-S cell without N,S-CDs decreased significantly (∼450 mAh g−1) at the higher discharge current densities of 2.0 C, whereas that of the Li-S cell with the N,S-CDs additive was ∼600 mA h g−1. The SEM images of the LMA with and without N,S-CDs additive added after rate testing were shown as Fig. 6
g-i. Clearly, the surface of LMA in the untreated electrolyte was more disorderly and agree with their poor electrochemical performance, albeit with the fact that there were some crack on the surface of LMA with N,S-CDs additives.

In conclusion, we proposed the N,S-CDs as an novel and efficient deposition regulating additive to suppress dendritic Li growth. The so-synthetic N,S-CDs dispersed well in conventional ether electrolyte and showed lithiophilic nature thanks to the presence of numerous functional group. Therefore, N,S-CDs enable to absorb surrounding Li+ and further co-deposit with Li+ ion during Li plating process, which was vividly illustrated by images of confocal laser scanning microscope. Therefore, a smooth and dense Li deposition layer was achieved with the beneficial effect of N,S-CDs. As expected, Li|Li symmetric cell with N,S-CDs exhibited a superior stability with lower voltage hysteresis during long-term cycling in strongly contrast with poor performance of the Li|Li symmetric cell within untreated electrolyte. Moreover, N,S-CDs can also significantly improve the capacity of the Li-S full cell from 491 to ∼600 mAh/g after 200 cycles at 1.0 C, which indicated the potential of our contributions for practical application of lithium metal battery.

The N,S co-doped carbon dots were obtained through a one-pot hydrothermal synthesis. Specifically, 2.0 g thiourea and 100 μL sulfuric acid were added in 20 mL acetaldehyde under constant sonication, and then the mixture was transferred into a Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180°C for 6 h. After cooling down to room temperature naturally, the obtained product was dispersed homogeneously in the deionized water via sonication, and then purified by filtered through membrane (0.22 µm pore size) and washed with deionized water several times to get rid of the impurities. After drying in the oven at 60°C for 12 h, the yellowish-brown N,S-CDs powder was obtained (Fig. S9, Supporting Information).

The sulfur-carbon cathode was prepared according to previous report [52]. Next, the pre-prepared sulfur-carbon composites, polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF) as well as super-P were ground evenly with weight ratio 8:1:1, and further dispersed well in N-methyl 2-pyrrolidone (NMP) under constantly stirring. The well-mixed slurry was coated onto Al foil and further dried under vacuum at 60°C for 24 h. The areal mass loading of the active material was ∼1.0 mg cm−2.

The UV-vis absorption of N,S-CDs solution was carried out on a Agilent UV-2450 spectrophotometer. The microstructural and surface state of N,S-CDs were characterized by typical transmission electron microscopy (JEM−2100F), X-ray diffraction (Rigaku D/max 2550 VB+ 18 kW, Cu Kα radiation), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Thermo Scientific Escalab 250Xi) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (AVTA-TAR, 370), respectively. The morphology evolution of Li+ ions deposition with and without N,S-CDs added were characterized by SEM (JEOL JSM-7610FPlus). In detail, the coin cells after different cycles were disassembled in the Ar-filled glove box and then the electrodes were rinsed to remove the residual electrolyte solute by 1,2-dimethoxyethane (DME) before observation. Besides, the cross-section morphology of the electrodes was obtained via the lancet slicing. The fluorescence images of lithium metal anode with and without N,S-CDs were recorded on a confocal laser scanning microscope (Leica TCS SP8, Germany) under the excitation of 458 nm with a 10x and 40x objective lens.

In an Ar-filled glove box (O2<0.01 ppm, H2O<0.01 ppm), the N,S-CDs powders were dispersed into 3.0 mL conventional ether electrolyte (1.0 M LiTFSI + 2.0 %wt LiNO3 in DME:DOL, v:v=1:1) with the target concentration of 0.1-0.8 mg mL−1, respectively. All of the electrochemical performance tests were carried out using standard CR2016-type coin cells with Celgard 2400 as the separator on battery testing system (LAND, Wuhan, China). Particularly, the Li|Cu half-cell were assembled with lithium foil anode as well as copper foil cathode for coulombic efficiency testing and then the Li|Cu half-cell were cycled within a voltage range of 0 to 1.0 V (vs. Li+/Li) at current densities of 0.5 and 1.0 mA cm−2. As for long-term cycling, the Li|Li symmetric cells were assembled with two lithium foils and then tested at the current densities at 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0 and 5.0 mA cm−2 with an areal capacity of 1.0 mAh cm−2. The exchange current density was obtained in Li|Li symmetric cell at various current densities from 20 to 120 μA at room temperature. Besides, Li-S full-cells were assembled with as-prepared sulfur cathode and lithium anode (thickness about 500 μm), and then the cell were tested within a voltage range of 1.7 to 2.8 V (vs. Li+/Li) at rate of 0.2, 0.5, 1.0 and 2.0 C (1.0 C=1675 mAh g−1). Moreover, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was tested on Solartron 1260 and 1287 with a frequency range from 10−2 to 105 Hz with the amplitude of 5.0 mV.

Density functional theory (DFT) calculation was carried out with the dmol3 module as implemented in the Materials Studios package of Accelrys Inc to obtain the binding energy between Li+ ions with each lithiophilic site (graphitic-N, pyrrolic-N, pyridinic-N and sulfur atom). For all calculations the cut-offs of the wave function are 400 eV. Each geometries of the Li ions with N,S-CDs bonded were optimized until the energy and force convergence criteria are 5.0 × 10−6 eV and 0.01 eV Å−1, respectively. Thereinto, the binding energy was defined as E(N,S-CDs-Li+)-E(N,S-CDs)-E(Li+), and it was calculated with the basis set superposition error corrected by the counterpoise method [36].
Shuo Li: Experiment, data curation, writing-original draft preparation.
Zheng Luo:Formal analysis.
Hanyu Tu: Validation.
Hao Zhang: Validation.
Weina Deng: Validation.
Guoqiang Zou: Software.
Hongshuai Hou: Conceptualization, supervision.
Xiaobo Ji: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52074359, 51904342), National Key Research and Development Program of China (2018YFB0104200), Hunan Provincial Science and Technology Plan (2020JJ3048), the Science and Technology Innovation Program of Hunan Province (2020RC4005, 2019RS1004), Innovation Mover Program of Central South University (2020CX007), and Fundamental Research Funds for Central Universities of the Central South University (2020zzts393).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.ensm.2021.08.008.
